name: Abbas Musawi
description: Shaykh and Secretary-General of Hezbollah, predecessor to Hassan Nasrallah. Killed by Israel in 1992.

name: Abu Nidal
description: Extremist Palestinian dissident and blood enemy of Arafat and the mainstream PLO. His followers shot Israeli Ambassador Argov in 1982, apparently under Iraqi orders, providing Israel's stated pretext for the invasion.

name: Al-Qwat al-Mushtaraka
description: Joint forces of the PLO and the Lebanese National Movement, led by Druze leader Kamal Junblat. Held key positions in the south, particularly in Tyre and the Rashidiyya camp.

name: Alain Pellegrini
description: French General; UNIFIL Commander 2004–February 2007. Continued in post when the mandate was enhanced and the mission renamed UNIFIL II in 2006.

name: Alberto Asarta Cuevas
description: Spanish General; UNIFIL Commander 2010–2012.

name: Amal
description: Lebanese Shia grassroots movement established in the early 1970s as the militia adjunct to Musa al-Sadr's Harakat al-Mahrumin; resurgent after 1978 and led by Nabih Berri. Clashed seriously with Palestinians from 1980–81 and served as the political origin of Hassan Nasrallah before he moved to Hezbollah.

sources.title: The Blue Helmets: A Review of United Nations Peace-Keeping (3rd ed.), Chapter 6: UNIFIL
(In)security Zones in South Lebanon
'Mr. UNIFIL' Reflects on a Quarter Century of Peacekeeping in South Lebanon
The Palestinian Resistance Movement in Lebanon 1967—82: Survival,Challenges, and Opportunities
New Rulesof the Game: Israel and Hizbollah after the Withdrawal from Lebanon(Chapters 1, 7 & Conclusion)

name: Amin Gemayel
description: Brother of Bashir Gemayel who succeeded him as Lebanese president following Bashir's assassination in September 1982. Presided over Lebanon during the period of the May 17 Agreement.

name: Antoine Lahad
description: Major General who replaced Sa'ad Haddad as SLA commander after Haddad's death in 1984. Tasked by Israel with maintaining security in the southern Lebanese buffer zone.

name: Anwar Sadat
description: Egyptian president whose November 1977 announcement of intent to visit Israel shattered Arab unity and collapsed broader Middle East peace negotiations, triggering bilateral Egyptian-Israeli talks.

name: Arab Deterrent Force (ADF)
description: Force established by the Arab League in November 1978 to help the Lebanese government reassert authority over its territory. Dominated in practice by Syrian troops already present in Lebanon; Israel vetoed its deployment south of the Litani River.

name: Arab League
description: Pan-Arab intergovernmental body that established the Arab Deterrent Force in November 1978. Sought to help Lebanon restore authority over its territory.

sources.title: The Culture of Peace and Peacekeeping Operations:Middle East Case Study (chapter in Cultural Challenges in Military Operations)

name: Arab Socialist Unions
description: At least three Lebanese gangs operating under this name, hiring out their services to any paying group, primarily Palestinians during the relevant period. Described as essentially mercenary in nature.

name: Ariel Sharon
description: Israeli Defense Minister at the time of the 1982 invasion of Lebanon. Key architect of the military operation alongside Prime Minister Begin.

name: Bashir Gemayel
description: Powerful Maronite commander of the Lebanese Forces militia, openly allied with Israel, who was elected Lebanese president. Assassinated in September 1982 before taking office.

sources.title: UNIFIL Operational Strategy: A Three-Pronged Approach

name: Believer's Resistance
description: Splinter group of Amal led by former Lebanese army intelligence sergeant Mustafa Dirani, sympathetic to Hezbollah. Responsible for the 1988 kidnapping of UN Observer Group Lebanon head Col. William Higgins.

name: Brian Urquhart
description: Head of UN peacekeeping operations during the early UNIFIL period. Key figure in managing UN peacekeeping policy in Lebanon.

name: Caspar Weinberger
description: U.S. Secretary of Defense who publicly criticized the May 17 Agreement, noting its secret side letter effectively gave Syrian President Assad veto power over any Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon.

name: Claudio Graziano
description: Italian General; UNIFIL Commander February 2007–January 2010. Based at UNIFIL HQ in Naqoura.

sources.title: UN Blue: Interdependence Between UN Peacekeeping and Peacemaking
At a Crossroads: Israeli Policy on UNIFIL
UN Blue: Interdependence Between UN Peacekeeping and Peacemaking

name: Daoud Sulaiman Daoud
description: Amal leader mentioned alongside Nabih Berri as a key figure in the movement.

sources.title: European Conflict Management in the Middle East: Toward a More Effective Approach

name: Egypt
description: Under President Sadat, broke Arab unity in 1977 by announcing intent to visit Israel, collapsing broader Middle East peace negotiations and triggering bilateral Egyptian-Israeli talks brokered by the U.S.

name: Elias Sarkis
description: Lebanese president whose swearing-in ostensibly ended the first phase of the civil war. Faced sustained attacks from right-wing Maronite factions, particularly Bashir Gemayel.

name: Emmanuel Erskine
description: Commander of UNIFIL during the Israeli occupation period post-1982. Oversaw UNIFIL's shift to humanitarian and relief functions.

sources.title: Soldiers of Peace: Estonia, Finland and Ireland in UNIFIL

name: European Union (EU)
description: Backbone of UNIFIL's TCC framework under the 1701 mandate, with two-thirds of international troops in southern Lebanon being European (Italy, France, Spain) as of 2009. Lebanon's main trading partner and largest donor as of 2009.

sources.title: Written Statement on Mass Exoduses and Displaced Persons (E/CN.4/2001/NGO/19)
The Blue Helmets: A Review of United Nations Peace-Keeping (3rd ed.), Chapter 6: UNIFIL
Soldiers of Peace: Estonia, Finland and Ireland in UNIFIL

name: France
description: Contributor to both UNIFIL and the Multinational Force (MNF) in 1982, and a leading European TCC under the 1701 mandate. Attempted to push a UN Security Council resolution for a stronger, unified UN force covering both southern Lebanon and Beirut, which was vetoed by the Soviet Union.

sources.title: Reconsidering the Struggle over UNIFIL in Southern Lebanon
Time to End UNIFIL's Mandate in Southern Lebanon?
On the Brink of Escalation: The UNIFIL Mandate Renewal is a Victim of the Beirut Explosion
Where Is the Israel-Hizbollah War Going?
Expanded United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL II)

name: Franco Angioni
description: Italian General; Commander of ITALCON, the Italian contingent within the Multinational Force (MNF) in Lebanon, 1982–1984. Oversaw the evacuation of Palestinian fighters and Syrian soldiers from besieged Beirut.

name: George Habash
description: Leader of the Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP), a Palestinian armed faction active in southern Lebanon.

name: George Shultz
description: U.S. Secretary of State who brokered the May 17 Agreement (1983) between Israel and Lebanon, later criticized by Defense Secretary Weinberger for its secret protocol giving Syria veto power over Israeli withdrawal.

name: Giandominico Picco
description: Representative of UN Secretary-General Pérez de Cuéllar. Negotiated directly with Hezbollah captors for the release of hostages.

name: Giovanni Ridinò
description: Italian General appointed head of the Strategic Military Cell (SMC) within DPKO New York in 2006, supporting the enhanced UNIFIL II mandate.

name: Giuseppe Guglietta
description: Italian Captain appointed Deputy Director of the Strategic Military Cell (SMC) in 2007. Tasked with supporting DPKO with military experts, linking New York to the field, and engaging key TCCs in strategic planning.

name: Guglielmo Luigi Miglietta
description: Italian General; former Commander of the Italian UNIFIL base in Chamaa. Exact dates of service not specified in source.

name: Hafez al-Assad
description: Syrian president who gained effective veto power over Israeli withdrawal from Lebanon via the secret protocol of the May 17 Agreement (1983). Spearheaded the Arab rejection front following Sadat's move toward bilateral peace with Israel.

name: Harakat al-Mahrumin (Movement of the Deprived)
description: Populist Shia reform movement founded by Musa al-Sadr in the early 1970s. Parent political movement of Amal.

name: Hassan Nasrallah
description: Leader of Hezbollah, succeeding Abbas Musawi after his assassination in 1992. Began his political career within Amal before moving to Hezbollah.

name: Hezbollah
description: Lebanese Shia armed movement and political party with strong Iranian influence, emerging as a major resistance force against Israeli occupation in southern Lebanon. Party to the April Understanding ceasefire; participated in 1992 parliamentary elections; suspected ties to the Revolutionary Justice Organization; conducted periodic reminder operations in the Shebaa Farms.

name: ITALCON
description: Italian military contingent deployed as part of the Multinational Force (MNF) in Lebanon, 1982–1984, comprising approximately 500 soldiers. Tasked with the evacuation of 12,000 Palestinian fighters and 3,000 Syrian soldiers from besieged Beirut; later reconstituted as part of MNF II following the Sabra and Shatila massacres.

sources.title: Where Is the Israel-Hizbollah War Going?
US Policy Towards Lebanon & Iran's Response (chapter in Lebanon at the Crossroads)
Hizballah of Lebanon: Extremist Ideals vs. Mundane Politics

Israel's Policy in South Lebanon
Soldiers of Peace: Estonia, Finland and Ireland in UNIFIL
Peacekeeping in Lebanon and Civilian Protection

name: Islamic Students Union
description: Local Shia resistance cells organized by Shaykh Raghib Harb around Nabatiya, structured in five- to six-man village cells. Mounted significant resistance against Israeli forces; Hezbollah attempted but largely failed to absorb the group when moving south.

name: Israel
description: Key military actor in southern Lebanon, conducting the 1978 and 1982 invasions and organizing the SLA proxy militia. Maintained a unilaterally declared security zone south of the Litani River and vetoed the Arab Deterrent Force's deployment in the south.

sources.title: Written Statement on Landmines/Lebanon (E/CN.4/2004/NGO/228)
Written Statement on Vulnerable Groups/Lebanon (E/CN.4/2001/NGO/20)

The South Lebanon Border Zone: A Local Perspective
A Primer on Lebanon: History, Palestine and Resistance to Israeli Violence
Dispatch from South Lebanon: Life as Resistance at the End of the World

name: Italy
description: Major TCC contributor to both the MNF (1982–1984) and UNIFIL II under the 1701 mandate, providing multiple Force Commanders including Graziano and Serra. Deployed ITALCON for the evacuation of Palestinian fighters from Beirut in 1982 and consistently one of the largest European contributors to UNIFIL.

sources.title: National prerogatives in multilateral peacekeeping: Italy in Lebanese perception and Rome's role within UNIFIL II
Expanded United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL II)
Hezbollah, the LAF and the Power of Perception (chapter in The Lebanese Armed Forces, Hezbollah and the Race to Defeat ISIS)

name: Javier Pérez de Cuéllar
description: UN Secretary-General whose representative Giandominico Picco conducted negotiations with Hezbollah over hostage kidnappings. Key figure in UN diplomacy regarding Lebanon.

name: Jimmy Carter
description: U.S. President whose plans for comprehensive Geneva Middle East peace talks collapsed in late 1977. Redirected efforts toward facilitating bilateral Egyptian-Israeli negotiations.

name: Kamal Junblat
description: Druze leader who led al-Qwat al-Mushtaraka, the joint PLO-Lebanese National Movement forces. Key figure in the Lebanese left-nationalist coalition during the civil war.

name: Lebanese Forces (LF)
description: Right-wing Maronite militia commanded by Bashir Gemayel, openly allied with Israel. Key player in the Lebanese civil war on the Christian right-wing side.

name: Lebanese Front
description: Coalition of right-wing Christian parties and militias that opposed the Lebanese National Movement during the civil war. Allied broadly with Israel and against the Palestinian armed presence.

name: Lebanese National Movement (LNM)
description: Coalition of leftist and nationalist Lebanese parties allied with and utilizing Palestinian armed groups during the civil war. Opposed the Lebanese Front.

sources.title: UNIFIL II: Emerging and Evolving European Engagement in Lebanon and the Middle East
Where Is the Israel-Hizbollah War Going?

A Primer on Lebanon: History, Palestine and Resistance to Israeli Violence

name: Lebanon Independent Border Assessment Team (LIBAT)
description: Mission dispatched by the UN Secretary-General in June 2007 to assess Lebanon's border security. Found it insufficient to prevent arms smuggling; a 2008 follow-up mission registered no significant progress, confirming the weapons embargo had not been effectively enforced.

sources.title: UNIFIL II, Israel, Lebanon, the UN and the International Community
European Conflict Management in the Middle East: Toward a More Effective Approach
The Success of UNIFIL: An Important Role in Security and Development Despite The Doubts
UNIFIL II: Emerging and Evolving European Engagement in Lebanon and the Middle East
Israel's Policy in South Lebanon

name: Maritime Task Force UNIFIL (MTF UNIFIL)
description: UNIFIL naval component tasked with monitoring Lebanon's territorial waters, securing the coastline, and preventing arms smuggling. Operates at the request of the Lebanese government; boarding of suspicious vessels, confiscation, and arrests are restricted to the Lebanese navy and customs, significantly limiting its interdiction capacity.

name: Menachem Begin
description: Israeli Prime Minister at the time of the 1982 invasion of Lebanon (Operation Peace for Galilee). Key decision-maker alongside Defense Minister Ariel Sharon.

name: Mohammed Husain Fadlallah
description: Senior Shia shaykh described as the enduring spiritual inspiration behind Hezbollah. His religious authority gave Hezbollah significant ideological grounding.

name: Muhammad Mahdi Shams al-Din
description: Prominent Shia thinker and religious scholar who led the Supreme Islamic Shia Council. Known for sponsoring ecumenical Christian-Muslim dialogue; the Council is regarded as an institutional rival to both Amal and Hezbollah.

name: Multinational Force (MNF)
description: U.S.-led force comprising American, French, and Italian troops established in August 1982 at Lebanon's request, without UNSC authorization. Mandated to supervise PLO withdrawal and restore Lebanese sovereignty in Beirut; doubled in size after the Sabra and Shatila massacres to serve as an interposition force (MNF II).

name: Musa al-Sadr
description: Iran-born Shia cleric who founded both the Harakat al-Mahrumin and its militia adjunct Amal in the early 1970s, and established the Supreme Islamic Shia Council in 1969. Disappeared enigmatically during a trip to Libya in 1978.

name: Mustafa Dirani
description: Former Lebanese army intelligence sergeant who led the Believer's Resistance, an Amal splinter group sympathetic to Hezbollah. Responsible for the 1988 kidnapping of UN Observer Group Lebanon head Col. William Higgins.

name: Nabih Berri
description: Head of the Amal movement and key Shia political figure. Became Speaker of the Lebanese Parliament after the end of the internal war in 1990.

sources.title: New Rulesof the Game: Israel and Hizbollah after the Withdrawal from Lebanon(Chapters 1, 7 & Conclusion)
The Blue Helmets: A Review of United Nations Peace-Keeping (3rd ed.), Chapter 6: UNIFIL
The Blue Helmets: A Review of United Nations Peace-Keeping (3rd ed.), Chapter 6: UNIFIL
(In)security Zones in South Lebanon
'Mr. UNIFIL' Reflects on a Quarter Century of Peacekeeping in South Lebanon
The Palestinian Resistance Movement in Lebanon 1967—82: Survival,Challenges, and Opportunities
The South Lebanon Border Zone: A Local Perspective

name: PLO (Palestine Liberation Organization)
description: Palestinian armed organization that made southern Lebanon its main theater of operations against Israel, drawing Lebanon into the Arab-Israeli conflict. Party to both the Cairo Agreement (1969) and the Chtaura Agreement (1977); Arafat served as overall interlocutor for Palestinian factions and their Lebanese allies.

name: Paolo Serra
description: Italian General; UNIFIL Commander from 2012 (as of source date). Also referenced as Commander of UNIFIL II.

name: Philip Habib
description: U.S. Ambassador who negotiated the PLO-Israel ceasefire following cross-border exchanges in July 1981.

name: Pierre Gemayel
description: Father of Bashir and Amin Gemayel; founding figure of the Maronite political establishment. Like his son Bashir, viewed Sa'ad Haddad as a traitor.

name: Popular Front for the Liberation of Palestine (PFLP)
description: Palestinian armed faction led by George Habash with a significant armed presence in southern Lebanon. Had a tough local commander, Major Nur, described as a legendary fighter.

sources.title: Time to End UNIFIL's Mandate in Southern Lebanon?
The Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire Was Built to Fail

name: Raghib Harb
description: Charismatic Shia shaykh based in Jibshit near Nabatiya who organized the Islamic Students Union resistance cells against Israeli forces. Hezbollah attempted but largely failed to absorb his network when it moved south.

name: Revolutionary Justice Organization
description: Group that claimed responsibility for the 1988 killing of Col. William Higgins. Widely believed to have ties to Hezbollah.

name: Sa'ad Haddad
description: Cashiered Lebanese Major recruited by Israel to command the South Lebanon Army proxy militia as a buffer against Palestinian commandos along Israel's northern border. Died of cancer in 1984.

name: Saudi Arabia
description: Established as one of the two main regional political players in Lebanon by the 1989 Taif Accord, alongside Syria.

sources.title: UN Blue: Interdependence Between UN Peacekeeping and Peacemaking
Reconsidering the Struggle over UNIFIL in Southern Lebanon
On the Brink of Escalation: The UNIFIL Mandate Renewal is a Victim of the Beirut Explosion
Expanded United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon (UNIFIL II)

name: Shlomo Argov
description: Israeli Ambassador seriously wounded in an assassination attempt in 1982 by followers of Abu Nidal. Israel used the attack as the stated pretext for launching Operation Peace for Galilee.

name: South Lebanon Army (SLA)
description: Israeli-organized proxy militia originally under cashiered Lebanese Major Sa'ad Haddad, later commanded by Major General Antoine Lahad after Haddad's death in 1984. Served as a buffer along Israel's northern border and was to be integrated into the security zone envisaged by the May 17 Agreement.

name: Spain
description: European TCC contributor to UNIFIL under the 1701 mandate. Provided Force Commander General Alberto Asarta Cuevas (2010–2012); among the leading European troop contributors alongside France and Italy.

sources.title: Gaining Local Legitimacy in Stabilization Operations: The Spanish Peacekeepers Case in Lebanon-UNIFIL II
UNIFIL II, Israel, Lebanon, the UN and the International Community

name: Strategic Military Cell (SMC)
description: Ad hoc cell established within the UN Department of Peacekeeping Operations in New York in 2006 to support UNIFIL II. Tasked with providing DPKO with a pool of military experts, strengthening links between New York and the field, and engaging key troop-contributing countries in strategic military planning.

name: Supreme Islamic Shia Council
description: Representative Shia body established by Musa al-Sadr in 1969 to place Shia on equal political footing with Sunnis. Led by Shams al-Din; active in ecumenical dialogue; regarded as institutional rival to both Amal and Hezbollah.

name: Syria
description: Deployed approximately 25,000 troops into Lebanon in June 1976, becoming the dominant component of the Arab Deterrent Force and making its military presence effectively permanent. Established as a main regional political player in Lebanon by the Taif Accord (1989); gained effective veto over Israeli withdrawal via the secret protocol of the May 17 Agreement.

sources.title: (In)security Zones in South Lebanon
Hizballah of Lebanon: Extremist Ideals vs. Mundane Politics
New Rulesof the Game: Israel and Hizbollah after the Withdrawal from Lebanon(Chapters 1, 7 & Conclusion)
The Israel-Hezbollah Ceasefire Was Built to Fail
Is a Strategic Change in Lebanon-Israel Relations Possible at the Present Time?

name: Tripartite Liaison Mechanism
description: Three-way military coordination body linking UNIFIL, the IDF, and the Lebanese Armed Forces (LAF), operating through real-time communications and monthly meetings at the UN base in Naqoura. Focused on Blue Line marking, infrastructure problem-solving, and de-escalation; credited as a critical non-kinetic tool for preventing conflict flare-ups, transmitting messages between parties, and maintaining calm on the ground.

name: Tufayli
description: Former Secretary-General of Hezbollah. Expelled from the organization in January 1998.

sources.title: New Rulesof the Game: Israel and Hizbollah after the Withdrawal from Lebanon(Chapters 1, 7 & Conclusion)

name: UN Observer Group Lebanon
description: Unit of approximately 50 men drawn from the UN Truce Supervision Organization, set up to monitor armistice lines and assist UNIFIL in southern Lebanon. Staff largely based in Israel; headed by Col. William Higgins until his kidnapping and killing in 1988.

sources.title: The UN Secretary-General's Periodic Report on Resolution 1701: Somewhat Revealing, Still Mostly Concealing

name: UN Truce Supervision Organization (UNTSO)
description: UN body established in 1949 to monitor armistice lines in the Middle East. Parent organization of the UN Observer Group Lebanon.

name: UNIFIL
description: United Nations Interim Force in Lebanon, deployed in southern Lebanon following Israel's 1978 invasion. Operated under extraordinary circumstances including direct Israeli occupation, focusing on emergency relief, humanitarian intervention, and record keeping when unable to perform peacekeeping work.

sources.title: Written Statement on Landmines/Lebanon (E/CN.4/2004/NGO/228)

US Policy Towards Lebanon & Iran's Response (chapter in Lebanon at the Crossroads)
A Primer on Lebanon: History, Palestine and Resistance to Israeli Violence

name: United States
description: Major external actor in the Lebanese conflict, brokering several key agreements including the 1981 PLO-Israel ceasefire, the May 17 Agreement (1983), and the April Understanding between Hezbollah and Israel. Led the Multinational Force (MNF) in 1982 and shifted to strong support for Israel's Lebanon narrative under the Reagan administration.

name: Uri Lubrani
description: Israeli coordinator of affairs in Lebanon. Served as a key liaison figure for Israeli interests in the Lebanese context.

name: Vasco Angelotti
description: Italian General; former Commander of the Italian UNIFIL base in Chamaa. Exact dates of service not specified in source.

name: William Higgins
description: U.S. Marine Lieutenant Colonel serving as head of the UN Observer Group Lebanon. Kidnapped in 1988 and killed by the Believer's Resistance, an Amal splinter group with Hezbollah ties.

name: Yasser Arafat
description: Chairman of the PLO and overall interlocutor for all Palestinian factions and their Lebanese allies prior to UNIFIL II. Key figure in Palestinian armed operations launched from southern Lebanon.
